segments.s033-g105: همه quantifies the noun phrase بند و نیرنگت ('all of your fetters and tricks'); the -ت on نیرنگت is the 2nd-person possessive 'your'. The preposition از introduces the cause or means ('by/through'), so از رنگ here means 'by (her) color/complexion/appearance' (رنگ = appearance). برد is the past 3rd-person form of بردن 'to take (away)' with its subject omitted (understood from the next clause), so the literal structure is 'all your bonds and tricks from (her) beauty he took.'
segments.s033-g106: دلارام is the explicit subject (a proper name). بگرفت is a classical past form (with the b- prefix) meaning 'seized' or 'took' and its object is omitted because it is understood from the narrative context. و simply connects the two past actions. گاهت is گاه 'time/occasion' with the 2nd-person possessive -ت 'your', and سپرد is the past of سپردن 'to entrust/hand over', taking گاهت as its object ('entrusted/handed over your time/fate').
segments.s033-g9: نشسته is a past participle used as a stative predicate (‘seated’). به آرام is an adverbial phrase (به + adjective) meaning ‘calmly / in calm’; در پیشگاه is a prepositional phrase ‘in the presence (of the sovereign/court)’. The whole segment gives the scene-setting: ‘seated calmly in the presence’.
segments.s033-g10: چو introduces a simile (‘like/as’). سرو بلند is the comparison ‘a tall cypress’ (suro‑baland). از marks origin/point of reference (‘from/of’), برش here functions as ‘her profile/side’ (poetic use of برش = section/profile). گرد is an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning ‘turned’ or ‘went round’ (here ‘turned away’), and ماه is ‘the moon’ (used as an image for a beautiful face). The clause therefore reads roughly ‘like a tall cypress; the moon turned away from her profile.’
segments.s033-g11: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’ or ‘out of’ introducing what is held/appears on one side. یک دست = ‘one hand’ (literal or idiomatic). سرو سهی is a poetic epithet, ‘the dark cypress’ (سرو = cypress; سهی = dark/poetic epithet). شهرناز is a proper name (Shahrnāz). The copula is omitted (typical in classical Persian): ‘from one hand (was) the dark cypress Shahrnāz.’
segments.s033-g12: به دست دگر is a locative/positional phrase ‘in the other hand’ (به + noun phrase). دگر = ‘other’. ماه‌روی is a compound adjective ‘moon‑faced’ (i.e. beautiful‑faced). ارنواز is a proper name (Arnavāz). Again the verb/copula is omitted; the phrase lists the counterpart held/standing in the other hand.
segments.s033-g13: همه شهر یک‌سر is an emphatic fronting meaning ‘the whole city, entirely’. پر از + noun means ‘full of …’ (پر = full (of)). لشکرش is ‘his army’ (لشکر + possessive suffix ش). The noun phrase states that the city was completely filled with his troops.
segments.s033-g14: کمربستگان is the plural participial/noun form ‘the girded ones’ (i.e. armed/ready warriors). صف زده is a compound participle meaning ‘having formed a line / drawn up in ranks’ (زده here participates as part of the compound). بر درش = ‘at his gate’ (بر = at/on/upon; در = gate; ش = his). The clause describes the armed men lined up at the gate.
segments.s033-g15: نه ... و نه is the correlative negation ‘neither ... nor’. آسیمه گشت is a verb phrase meaning ‘became distressed/distraught’ (آسیمه = distraught; گشت = became). پرسید راز = ‘asked the secret’ (پرسید = he asked; راز = secret). Together: ‘He neither became distraught nor asked about the secret.’
segments.s033-g16: نیایش‌کنان is a gerund/participle meaning ‘praying / in prayer’. رفت is simple past ‘he went’. بردش نماز here means ‘he took/performed his prayer’ (بردش = carried/took it; نماز = ritual prayer) — a concise poetic expression for going off to perform prayer. The sequence describes him leaving while engaged in devotions and offering the prayer.
segments.s033-g25: بفرمود is an honorific past-tense verb meaning 'he said/commanded' placed before the noun phrase; شاه دلاور دلاور is the noun phrase 'the brave king' (شاه = king; دلاور = brave/valiant). بدوی here is an epithet attached to the king (an older/formal adjective); the whole chunk is the subject + reporting verb 'he commanded'.
segments.s033-g26: که introduces a subordinate clause that reports the content of the command ('that / “…”'). رو functions here as a directional preposition 'toward' (classical usage), followed by آلت تخت شاهی, a genitive/nominal phrase 'the instrument/implements (i.e., regalia) of the royal throne'; بجوی is the present/imperfective imperative/subjunctive 'seek/search (for)' with an omitted 2nd-person subject.
segments.s033-g27: This is a sequence of imperatives. نبیذ آر is an imperative instruction to make/prepare/bring wine (آر here is an archaic verb-form meaning 'arrange/prepare/ornament'); و connects the two commands; رامشگران را بخوان is 'call/summon the ramashgarān (entertainers/minstrels)' with را marking them as the definite object and بخوان as an imperative ('cause to sing/let sing' or 'summon').
segments.s033-g28: بپیمای is an imperative formed from پیمود/پیما meaning here 'measure out / fill' and governs جام 'cup' (i.e. 'fill the cup'); و links the second imperative; بیارای is an imperative meaning 'adorn/decorate' (here applied to the banquet), and خوان is the noun 'banquet/table' that is to be adorned.
segments.s033-g29: کسی 'someone/anyone' begins the relative clause; کاو is an archaic relative pronoun equivalent to 'که او' ('who/that he') — it functions as 'who' here rather than its modern lexical senses; به رامش is a prepositional phrase 'worthy of / for solace' and سزای من است is a predicate 'is the deserving one for me' (سزا = desert/recompense; است = copula). Word order is subject–(relative)–prepositional phrase–predicate.
segments.s033-g30: به دانش here works adverbially 'by/through knowledge' or 'in learning'; همان is a demonstrative 'that very / the same'; دلزدای is a participial/adjectival form meaning 'one who causes displeasure/repels the heart' (i.e. 'off‑putting' or 'heart‑alienating'); من است closes the clause with the copula 'is mine' or 'is mine (in that role)'.
segments.s033-g31: Two imperatives again: بیار 'bring' governs انجمن 'assembly/meeting', and کن 'do, make' is the second imperative that combines with انجمن to mean 'bring together / hold an assembly'; بر تخت من is a prepositional phrase 'upon my throne' specifying where the assembly should be held.
segments.s033-g32: چنان چون is a classical double correlative meaning 'so / such as'; بود is the past copula 'was' (here used to state how it should be); در خور بخت من is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'befitting my bāxt/fortune' (در خور = 'befitting, worthy of'); together the line says the assembly should be 'such as befits my fortune.'
segments.s033-g33: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is past simple 'he heard'. از او marks source 'from him'. این سخن is the direct object 'this speech' (این = 'this', سخن = 'speech'). کدخدای names the person who heard (the headman) and follows the object here for poetic word order.
segments.s033-g34: بکرد is the past tense 'he did' (آورد/کرد type past). آنچه is a relative pronoun 'that which/what'. گفتش is 'said to him' (literally 'said‑him'), and بدو is the dative 'to him'; together گفتش بدو = 'the guide said to him'. رهنمای 'guide/adviser' names who gave the instruction. The whole phrase modifies بکرد: 'he did what the guide had told him.'
segments.s033-g35: آورد is past 'brought'. می‌روشن precedes the verb as a nominal/epithet meaning 'the illuminating/bright ones' (mi‑ prefix here is poetic; treat the whole as the thing brought). و links another coordinated noun رامشگران 'comforters/peacemakers/entertainers' as a second item brought.
segments.s033-g36: همان 'the same' refers back to the items just mentioned. در خورش 'in his dish/at his table' (در = 'in', خورش = 'dish/food' in classical usage). با introduces accompaniment 'with', and گهر مهتران is a genitive phrase 'the gems/jewels of the chiefs' (گهر = 'gem', مهتران = 'the great/chiefs').
segments.s033-g37: فریدون is the subject. غم افکند is an idiomatic past construction: غم (object 'grief') + افکند ('cast') = 'cast off sorrow'. و connects the next clause. رامش گزید = 'chose repose/peace' (رامش = 'tranquility', گزید = past 'chose'). Both verbs are simple past.
segments.s033-g38: شبی is an adverbial 'one night' or 'by night'. کرد here is past 'made/held' and takes جشنی 'a feast' as its object. چنان چون ... سزید is a fixed comparative/qualifying phrase meaning 'such as befit(ed)' — چنان/چون = 'such/as', and سزید (past of سزایدن) = 'was fitting/merited'.
segments.s033-g39: چو is the temporal 'when'. شد is past 'became'. رام گیتی is a predicate 'the world [became] tame/peaceful' (رام = 'tame/peaceful', گیتی = 'world'). دوان is a verb 'ran/hurried' (poetic form), and کندرو (lit. 'frankincense') here refers to incense or those carrying it (a common poetic shorthand), so the clause means 'the incense‑bearers ran about' or 'the frankincense ran' in the lively, figurative sense.
segments.s033-g40: برون آمد is the past compound 'came forth/stepped out' (برون = 'out/forth', آمد = 'came'). از پیش سالار نو is a prepositional phrase 'from before the new chief/commander' (از پیش = 'from the presence of', سالار نو = 'the new lord').
segments.s033-g49: از آن سه (literally “from those three”) is an ablative/genitive phrase meaning “of those three.” یکی is the indefinite/numberal subject “one.” کهتر is an adjectival predicate meaning “smaller/younger” and follows the subject (classical word order). اندر میان is a locative phrase “among/in the midst of,” placed after the adjective to specify where he is smaller/younger.
segments.s033-g50: به بالای is a prepositional phrase marking height/degree: “to the height of / lofty as.” سرو is used poetically (literally “cypress”), describing great height or elegance. و links the next phrase. به چهر کیان uses به + noun to mean “in/with” a quality — here “with the face/countenance of kings” (چهر = face/appearance, کیان = kings). The whole string lists qualities of that one man.
segments.s033-g51: به سال است is an idiomatic classical expression meaning “in years he is” (i.e., with respect to age). کهتر is the predicate adjective “younger.” فزونیش is a nominalized/possessive form meaning “his abundance/superiority,” and بیش (postposed) intensifies it as “more/greater,” creating the contrast “though younger, his greatness is greater.”
segments.s033-g52: از آن مهتران is an ablative/reference phrase “than those elders/greater ones.” او is the subject “he.” نهد is the classical present/simple-form verb “places/sets” (here narrative-present). پای پیش is an idiom “foot forward” meaning to step before or go ahead; together the clause means he steps forward in front of those elders.
segments.s033-g53: یکی گرز دارد is straightforward: numeric/indefinite subject + object + دارد = “one has/possesses a mace.” چو introduces a simile “as/like,” and یک لخت کوه is the image “a single bare mountain” (یک = one/emphatic, لخت = bare/plain, کوه = mountain) comparing either the mace’s bulk or the man’s bulk to a lone mountain.
segments.s033-g54: همی is the classical progressive/emphatic particle (often marking repeated or ongoing action). تابد (classical present of تابیدن) here means “shines/gleams” (the mace or the man gleams). اندر میان گروه is the locative “in the midst of the group/troop,” telling where the shining occurs.
segments.s033-g55: به اسپ is an instrumental/adverbial phrase “by/ on horse” (here “on horseback”). اندر آمد is the classical intransitive perfective/past narrative “came in/entered.” به ایوان شاه marks the destination with به + noun “into the king’s iwan/porch/hall.” Word order is classical: adverbial (by horse) + verb (came in) + goal.
segments.s033-g56: دو پرمایه is a noun phrase “two substantial/stalwart (men)” (پرمایه used as an epithet meaning well-endowed/wealthy or strong). با او means “with him” (accompanying him); همیدون is given as a proper name apposed or specified as the one who is with him; براه is an adverbial “on the road / en route.” The terse classical order places the accompaniment and name after the numeral phrase.
segments.s033-g73: چنین داد پاسخ بدو is a short clause meaning 'thus gave an answer to him.' چنين (thus) fronted for emphasis, داد (gave) is the past simple verb, پاسخ is the direct object 'answer,' and بدو (= به + او) is the prepositional phrase 'to him.' کندرو follows as a single word appended to the clause (here left as a quoted word/name/term); its exact function is nominal (see senses).
segments.s033-g74: که introduces reported speech or content ('that' / 'and said that'). آری is an interjection 'indeed/yes.' شنیدم is perfective 'I heard' (speaker reports having heard). تو is the vocative/emphatic 'you,' and پاسخ شنو is an imperative construction: پاسخ = 'answer' as the object and شنو is the imperative 'listen!' addressing 'you.'
segments.s033-g75: گر is the conditional conjunction 'if.' این نامور هست is a nominative phrase 'this renowned one is' with هست as the copula (present). مهمان تو is a genitival phrase 'your guest' (مهمان governed by تو). Word order is typical Persian: conditional + subject + copula + predicate.
segments.s033-g76: چه کارستش is an idiomatic fused form for 'what business is he' (چه کار + است + -ش). کارستش here functions as 'what (kind of) business/role he has.' اندر شبستان تو literally 'in your bedchamber' with اندر = 'in/within' and شبستان = 'bedchamber/inner chamber.' The clause asks rhetorically why such a person would be in the private chamber.
segments.s033-g77: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that.' با + دختران جهاندار جم means 'with the daughters of Jam, the world-ruler.' جهاندار is an epithet modifying جم; the whole noun phrase expresses accompaniment ('with Jam's daughters').
segments.s033-g78: نشیند is a present/habitual third-person singular 'he sits' (narrative present). زند رای is a verbal phrase meaning 'frames/sets counsel' (رای = counsel/plan is the object); here زند is the verb element forming 'to make/establish' (archaic/poetic usage). بر بیش و کم = 'on more and less,' an idiom meaning 'about matters great and small.'
segments.s033-g79: به یک دست is a prepositional phrase 'with one hand' (به + numeral + noun). گیرد is the present-tense third-person singular 'he takes/seizes.' رخ شهرناز is a possessive/genitival phrase 'the face of Shahrnaz' (proper name after the noun), so the whole clause describes a simultaneous action performed with one hand.
segments.s033-g80: به دیگر (literally 'with the other') implies 'with the other hand' elliptically. عقیق لب is a descriptive compound 'agate-lipped' (عقیق = agate, لب = lip), and ارنواز is the woman's name, so the phrase means 'the agate-lipped Arnavaz' (object of the same verb گیرد, omitted here by ellipsis).
segments.s033-g57: This is a short past action clause with an omitted subject (third person). بیآمد functions as a past verb ‘came’ (archaic/poetic form) followed by the prepositional phrase به تخت کئی بر نشست. تخت کئی is a noun + epithet (‘the Kayanian throne’), and بر نشست is a compound verbal idea 'sat upon' (بر = on/upon placed before نشست 'sat'). Word order is Verb + prepositional phrase + locative/goal + verbal particle, typical of older Persian poetic inversion.
segments.s033-g58: همه بند و نیرنگ تو is the object phrase ('all the bonds and stratagems of yours'), with تو marking possession. This is followed by کرد (past 'made/did') and the predicate adjective پست ('base/low'), so the construction means 'he made (them) base' or 'he reduced all your bonds and tricks to baseness.' Note the object-before-verb order and کرد used as a light verb meaning 'caused/made.'
segments.s033-g59: هر آن کس که بود is a relative clause meaning 'whoever was' or 'any person who was'; هر آن کس is an emphatic indefinite 'whichever person' and که بود is the relative + past 'was.' اندر ایوان تو is the locative phrase 'in your hall,' with اندر ('in/within') preceding the noun, a poetic/archaic placement.
segments.s033-g60: This segment lists two classes introduced by the repeated preposition ز ('from/among'): ز مردان مرد (literally 'from the men of men' — an emphatic genitive meaning the foremost/heroic men) و ز دیوان تو ('and from your diwan/diwans'). The repeated ز stresses membership ‘from among X and from among Y.’ مردان مرد is an intensifying plural + epithet construction.
segments.s033-g61: This is a vivid past result clause. سر از پای یک‌سر فرو ریختشان literally orders the elements: 'head from foot entirely down fell them.' یک‌سر is an adverb 'completely/entirely,' فرو is an adverbial intensifier 'downwards,' and ریخت with the pronominal suffix -شان is a past verb meaning 'fell' or 'were cast' referring back to the people mentioned earlier. The suffix -شان marks the object/affected people ('them'). Poetic inversion places the parts for emphasis.
segments.s033-g62: همه مغز با خون برآمیختشان is another past-stative clause: همه مغز ('all the brains') + با خون ('with blood') + برآمیختشان (past 'were mixed' / 'he mixed them') with -شان again referring to those bodies. The preposition با marks the instrument/medium ('with blood'). The overall sense is passive/resultant: 'their brains were mingled with blood.'
segments.s033-g63: بدو گفت ضحاک is an inverted clause 'to him said Zahhak' where بدو ('to him') marks the indirect object and گفت is the past 'said' (poetic ordering places the indirect object before the verb). شاید بدن begins the reported speech: شاید is a modal 'perhaps' and بدن here is the noun 'body/corpse' used elliptically ('perhaps (he is) a corpse').
segments.s033-g64: This continues the reported speech. که introduces the clause (here roughly 'for/because' or 'that'), مهمان بود شاد is a past clause 'the guest was merry' (مهمان = guest; بود = was; شاد = merry), and باید بدن places باید ('must/ought') before بدن ('the body/corpse'), yielding a somewhat elliptical construction meaning 'one must [do something] with the body' — the verb is implicit in poetry. The line therefore presents Zahhak's conjecture and his consequent judgment in a compressed, elliptical form.
segments.s033-g41: نشست is a past-tense 3rd-person verb here used in the sense 'took a seat / mounted' (classical usage can mean both 'sat' and 'set out on a mount'). از بر is a compound prepositional idea meaning 'from/on the back of' or 'off of', so از بر باره راه‌جوی groups as a prepositional phrase describing where he sat (باره = 'load, bundle' = the traveler's pack; راه‌جوی = 'traveller/seeker'). Word order is Verb + prepositional phrase, typical of classical Persian narration.
segments.s033-g42: سوی is a directional preposition 'toward'; شاه ضحاک is the noun phrase 'King Zahhak' in the object position of that preposition. بنهاد is a past 3rd-person verb 'placed/put' used idiomatically with روی (بنهادِ روی) to mean 'set/turned (his) face'. روی here is the direct object meaning 'face'.
segments.s033-g43: چو … رسید is a temporal clause 'when/just as … he reached'. بیآمد here functions as an adverbial/prehse — an archaic/poetic element meaning something like 'no sooner' or 'suddenly/without delay' (the listed senses are limited, but context shows it modifies the timing). پیش سپهبد means 'in the presence of/before the spahbed' and رسید is the past 3rd-person verb 'arrived/reached'.
segments.s033-g44: سراسر is an adverb 'throughout / at length' modifying the reporting verb بگفت (past 'he said / he told'). آنچه is the relative pronoun 'that which / whatever' governing the following verbs. دید و شنید are perfect/past participial senses 'he had seen and heard' (here they list simple forms; grammatically they complete what he 'told'). The conjunction و joins the two complements.
segments.s033-g45: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (به + او), marking the indirect object or addressee. گفت is the past-tense reporting verb 'he said' (the provided sense list is lexical but context shows it is the verb گفتن). کای is the vocative particle 'O' calling attention; شاه is the vocative noun 'king'; گردنکشان is a descriptive plural noun 'the neck-raisers', i.e. 'rebels' or 'proud/refractory ones' addressed or referred to.
segments.s033-g46: به برگشتن is a genitive/infinitival phrase 'concerning/with regard to (their) turning/returning' (به + verbal noun برگشتن). کارت is the possessive form 'your affair/work' (کار + ـت). آمد is the past verb 'came/appeared' and نشان is the noun 'sign / token' — together indicating 'a sign of their return has come to (your) business' or more loosely 'signs of their return have appeared.'
segments.s033-g47: سه مرد is the counted noun phrase 'three men'; سرافراز is an adjective 'proud / triumphant / lofty' modifying مرد; با introduces an accompanying instrument/party 'with'; لشکری is an adjectival/noun form meaning 'an army / troops' (here 'with an army'). The phrase places the three men together with their force.
segments.s033-g48: فراز آمدند is a compound verbal phrase 'came up / ascended / arrived on high' (فراز here emphasizes upward or forward arrival) with آمدند the past 3rd-plural 'they came'. از دگر کشوری is a prepositional phrase 'from another country/realm' (از = from; دگر = another; کشوری = of the country/realm). Word order places the source after the verb, typical in classical narrative.
segments.s033-g81: شب تیره‌گون is a noun phrase 'the dark-hued night'. خود is an emphatic/reflexive pronoun ('itself' / 'himself') that stresses the noun. بتر is a comparative/adjective meaning 'worse' and combines with زین ('saddle') to make a comparison; کند is the 3rd‑person singular present/historical narrative verb 'he/it makes/does'. The clause therefore says figuratively that the night itself is made 'worse' (or 'more dark') in comparison to the following image.
segments.s033-g82: به زیر سر is a prepositional phrase 'under the head' (i.e. beneath his head). از + مشک marks the material/origin 'of musk' (a scented material), with بالین 'pillow' as the noun made of that material. کند is again the 3rd‑person verb 'he places/makes' — together: 'he places a musk pillow under his head.'
segments.s033-g83: چو here means 'like/as' and introduces a simile. مشک is the tenor of the simile 'like musk'. آن دو گیسوی دو ماه تو is a nested noun phrase: آن دو گیسوی = 'those two locks of hair', with دو ماه تو an appositive 'your two moons' (a poetic epithet). Word order places the simile marker before the described image.
segments.s033-g84: که is the relative pronoun 'which/that' referring back to 'those two locks'. بودند is the past plural form 'they were'. همواره 'always/ever' modifies the predicate دلخواه تو, where دلخواه is a nominal predicate 'beloved/desired' and تو marks the possessor 'your'. The clause means 'which had always been your delight.'
segments.s033-g85: بگیرد is a 3rd‑person present/historical-narrative verb 'he takes/seizes'. به برشان literally 'to/upon them' here means 'to his bosom / into his arms' (بر = chest/upper body; -شان = 'them'). چو شد نیم مست is a temporal clause 'when he became half-drunk' (چو = 'when'; شد = 'became'; نیم مست = 'half‑intoxicated').
segments.s033-g86: بدین گونه ('in this manner') is an adverbial phrase; here the tokenization splits ب + دین but together mean 'in this'. مهمان 'guest' is the topic, and نباید is the impersonal negative modal 'one should not / it is not proper'. به دست literally 'by hand' or 'with the hand' idiomatically means 'handled/touched' — so the whole clause: 'A guest should not be handled this way.'
segments.s033-g87: برآشفت is a past narrative verb 'he became angry' (literally 'was stirred up'). ضحاک is the subject name. برسان is a causative/causal‑action verb 'he caused to bring / he sent', and کرگ is the thing sent — a bird (a dialectal bird name). The sequence is subject–verb–object in narrative style: 'Zahhak was enraged and sent (the) bird.'
segments.s033-g88: شنید is past 'he heard'. آن سخن 'that speech/those words' is the direct object. The following relative clause (کآرزو کرد مرگ) contains آرزو کرد 'expressed a wish' and مرگ 'death' as its object: 'which had wished/asked for death.' Grammatically, ک(=که) introduces the relative clause and کرد is the past verb 'did/expressed'.
segments.s033-g17: بر او آفرین کرد is an idiomatic classical Persian clause meaning “he uttered praise of him.” بر is the preposition linked to آفرین کردن (to praise); او is the person praised; آفرین is a noun/exclamation (“praise, acclamation”) and کرد is the past tense of کردن used here to form the verbal phrase “uttered/praised.” کای شهریار is a vocative: کای (archaic poetic 'O') + شهریار ('king').
segments.s033-g18: همیشه بزی تا بود روزگار expresses a wish/blessing. همیشه 'always'; بزی here is a poetic subjunctive/optative from زیستن meaning 'may you live' (this particular lexical sense is not in the provided list, so marked -1). تا introduces the temporal limit 'as long as/until'; بود is the copula ('be/exist') and روزگار means 'time/the world' — together: 'may you live always, as long as time endures.'
segments.s033-g19: خجسته نشست is a noun phrase 'auspicious seat/assembly' (خجسته = 'auspicious/blessed', نشست here functions as a noun 'seat/assembly'); تو is the vocative 'you' addressing the addressee; با + فرهی ('with nobility' / 'with noble bearing') qualifies the address. Word order is poetic; the phrase wishes the auspicious seat to be associated with the addressee.
segments.s033-g20: که introduces the reason/explanation clause ('for/since'); هستی here functions syntactically as the copular present 'you are' (classical 2nd-person form), followed by سزاوار 'deserving/fit' and شاهنشهی 'of imperial kingship' — together: 'for you are deserving of imperial rule.'
segments.s033-g21: جهان هفت کشور is a classical binomial 'the world — the seven countries/realms' (subject phrase). تو را uses را as the object/benefactive marker ('to you' / 'yours'); بنده باد is an optative expression 'may (they) be servants' where بنده = 'servant' and باد is the optative particle 'may'. The whole line is a blessing: 'May the seven realms be your servants.'
segments.s033-g22: سرت ('your head', possessive -ت suffix) is the subject; برتر 'higher/superior' is a comparative predicate; از + ابر بارنده 'than the rain‑bearing cloud' forms the comparison. باد again is the optative particle 'may' — 'May your head be loftier than the rain‑bearing cloud.'
segments.s033-g23: فریدونش is the proper name 'Fereydun' with an enclitic -ش (to him/his) fused in the line — here meaning 'to Fereydun' as the indirect object. فرمود is the past tense 'he said/commanded' (here with a sense of ordering). تا introduces a subordinate clause expressing purpose/result ('so that/that'); رفت is the past 'he went'; پیش means 'forward/in the presence (of someone)' — together: 'He told/commanded Fereydun to go forward.'
segments.s033-g24: بکرد is a past tense verbal compound 'he made/did' here meaning 'he made (it) clear' or 'he revealed'; آشکارا 'openly'; همه 'all'; راز خویش = 'his secret' where خوی (root) plus the enclitic ش = 'his' (خویش) gives the reflexive/possessive sense. The clause means 'he openly revealed all his secret.' Note the archaic placement of بکرد before its object.
segments.s033-g97: چو functions here as the conditional 'if' (classical/poetic use). بی‌بهره is an adjectival phrase 'deprived, lacking of'; باشی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive form 'you are' (used in the protasis of the conditional). ز governs the following noun phrase 'ز گاه مهی' = 'from/of the moon-hour' (i.e. the auspicious/moon-timed moment).
segments.s033-g98: مرا را = 'to me' / object marking (here split orthographically as م + را); کارسازندگی is a noun meaning the means/efficacy for accomplishing a task (literally 'work‑making/efficacy'); چون is interrogative 'how' here introducing the question about manner; دهی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive of دادن 'you give' (so together 'how will you give me ...').
segments.s033-g99: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun 'you' (vocative/subject). نسازی contains the negative prefix ن‑ + سازي (ساختن 'to make'), so it means 'you do not / you will not make' or 'why don’t you make' in exhortatory/critical sense. همی is a classical aspectual particle indicating continuous/ongoing action ('always/still' / here softening the admonition). کار خویش = 'your own work' (کار 'work' + خویش 'one's own', where the following enclitic ش marks possession).
segments.s033-g100: که introduces the explanatory clause 'for/since'. هرگز is the adverb 'never'. نیامدت is a negative past/perfective form (نـ + آمدت) meaning 'did not come to pass' / 'has not happened' (classical phrasing). از این کار پیش = 'from this work before' = 'in regard to this undertaking previously' (پیش = previously/before).
segments.s033-g101: ز introduces origin 'from'. تاج بزرگی = 'the crown (of) greatness' (تاج + بزرگی, a genitive/qualifying phrase). چو here means 'like/as'. موی از خمیر is a simile 'a hair from dough' (image of something thin/insubstantial extruding from a mass). The order is poetic (origin → simile).
segments.s033-g102: برون آمدی is the 2nd-person past/preterite form 'you came forth/you emerged' (classical word order places the verb after the adverb). مهترا is a vocative (addressing a woman, 'Mahtara' / 'radiant moon-lady'). چاره گیر is an imperative phrase 'take/find a remedy' (چاره = remedy; گیر here is imperative/causative sense 'take/seize' forming the command).
segments.s033-g103: تو را places 'you' in the object/topic position with the definite object marker را ('an enemy came upon you'). دشمن آمد is 'an enemy came'. به گه = 'at the time / on the occasion' (به + گه); برنشست is the past verb 'he sat (upon / took his place)' — classical compound verb with بر‑ prefix.
segments.s033-g104: یکی = 'one' / 'a certain (man)'. گرزه is 'mace' (weapon). گاوپیکر is a descriptive compound 'gavr‑pikar' meaning literally 'cow‑bodied' used figuratively for something huge/ox-like; here it qualifies the mace as very massive. به دست = 'in (his) hand' (instrumental expression). The whole phrase describes the attacker bearing a huge mace.
segments.s033-g89: Two prepositional phrases in parallel: به + noun works adverbially here as 'with' or 'by means of'. دشنام زشت is 'vile/ugly curses' (noun + adjective), and به آواز سخت is 'with a harsh voice/cry' (literally 'to/with voice harsh'). The coordinate و simply links the two instruments.
segments.s033-g90: شگفتی is the object/noun 'astonishment' or 'wonder'; بشورید is a plural imperative/jussive — here poetically meaning 'let ... boil/ surge' (command to cause agitation). با + شور بخت is an instrumental phrase 'with the (tumult/ardor) of fate' where شور qualifies the manner and بخت is its possessor.
segments.s033-g91: بدو گفت is a short clause 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto him' as an indirect object; گفت is the verb 'said' — a past narration). هرگز تو در خان من places an adverb of negation 'never' before the subject 'you', followed by a locative phrase 'in my khan/house'.
segments.s033-g92: از این پس is a temporal phrase 'from this (time) on / henceforth'. نباشی is a jussive/subjunctive form (negative of باشی) meaning 'you shall not be' or 'do not be' addressed to 'تو' above. نگهبان من is the predicate noun 'my guardian' (object/complement). Word order is typical: temporal phrase + verb + predicate.
segments.s033-g93: چنین داد پاسخ ورا پیشکار is a compact reporting clause: چنین داد پاسخ = 'thus gave (an) answer'; ورا = 'to him' (indirect object); پیشکار is the agent 'the attendant/steward' placed after the clause (poetic word order). داد is the past verb 'gave'.
segments.s033-g94: که is the complementizer introducing the attendant's speech ('that' / 'that is:'). ایدون is an archaic/poetic adverb meaning roughly 'now' or 'henceforth' (appearing in older Persian). گمانم here functions as a charge about belief/assumption addressed to the addressee (literally 'my thought' or idiomatically 'do not suppose'); من‌ای شهریار is vocative 'O king' with the speaker referencing himself implicitly—poetic compression makes the clause elliptical.
segments.s033-g95: کز آن بخت is a causal/relative prepositional phrase 'from/because of that fate' (کز = 'from/that which'); هرگز is the negator 'never'; نباشدت is a jussive/optative form meaning 'may it not be / you will not be' — an older/formal verbal form, here stating impossibility; بهر is 'for' or 'for the sake of'.
segments.s033-g96: به من is 'to/for me' (benefactive dative). چون here works as 'such (that)' or 'in such a way' introducing the subordinate idea, and دهی is a present/subjunctive form of دادن 'you give' ('that you give'). کدخدایی شهر is the object: 'the headmanship (kādkhudāʾī) of the city' (کدخدایی = the office of headman). The whole phrase means 'such that you give me the city's headship'.
segments.s033-g1: چو here is a temporal conjunction 'when'. The clause follows subject + preposition phrase + verb + predicate order: کشور (the realm) + ز ضحاک ('from/of Zahhâk') + بودی (poetic past 'was') + تهی (predicative adjective 'empty/devoid'). Together: 'when the realm was empty of Zahhâk.'
segments.s033-g2: This is a noun phrase describing a person: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain', followed by the adjective مایه‌ور 'wealthy'. بد به سان رهی is a coined descriptive simile: بد ('bad/evil' or 'ill‑disposed') + به سان ('in the manner of/like') + رهی ('a wayfarer'); the whole appositive means roughly 'a certain wealthy man, bad (or ill‑fated) like a traveller.' Note the postpositive adjectives and the fixed simile به سان.
segments.s033-g3: که introduces a relative clause ('who' / 'that') modifying the preceding person. او is the third‑person pronoun 'he'; داشتی is a poetic/archaic past form meaning 'had' (functioning here as third person past). The list گنج و تخت و سرای are the direct objects ('treasure and throne and house').
segments.s033-g4: شگفتی is a nominal/qualifying word 'wondrous' or 'marvels' describing those possessions. به here marks the cause or source ('by/through'), and دلسوزگی کدخدای is a genitive phrase 'the compassion/pity of the kadkhodâ (village headman)'. Together it says these possessions were wondrous thanks to the headman's pity.
segments.s033-g5: ورا is an object/locative pronoun 'to/for it' (or 'to him/it'). کندرو is the word being named (here used as a proper name/term). خواندندی is an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning 'called/named' (verbal adjective/participle or past form); بنام ('by name' / 'called') reinforces that it was given that name. Word order is poetic: object/pronoun + term + verb + the phrase 'بنام'.
segments.s033-g6: This clause describes motion/tone: به کندی (به + کندی) means 'with slowness' or 'slowly' (کندی = slowness/slow), زدی is a past tense verb 'struck/advanced/took' in poetic usage and here functions as 'he took/advanced', پیش بیداد گام is a prepositional phrase 'a step toward injustice/oppression' (پیش = toward/in front of, بیداد = injustice, گام = step). The whole clause: 'he moved slowly, taking a step toward oppression.'
segments.s033-g7: The phrase places the place first: به کاخ اندر آمد = 'came into the palace'. آمد is the past 'came' and دوان (literally 'running' or 'rushing') describes the manner. The proper name/term کندرو appears as the subject; the poetic order places the manner and name after the verb: 'Kandro came running into the palace.'
segments.s033-g8: در ایوان is locative 'in the iwan/portico'; یکی تاجور is 'a certain merchant' (یکی = a certain, تاجور = merchant/trader). دید is the past 'saw' and نو qualifies the merchant as 'new' or 'recent'. Word order is straightforward: location + noun phrase + verb + adjective.
segments.s033-g65: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the clause. داد is the past-tense verb 'gave/said' (poetic past), and پاسخ is its direct object 'answer' placed before the verb (a common poetic/object-before-verb order). ورا is the enclitic object 'to him' (i.e. 'to the steward') and پیشکار names that addressee ('the steward/attendant').
segments.s033-g66: که introduces the content clause ('that ...'). مهمان is the subject 'the guest'. ابا is the classical verb here meaning 'refused / was reluctant' (past or perfective sense). گرزه گاوسار is the object phrase 'the mace/cudgel of the ox‑herd' (گرزه = 'mace', گاوسار = possessor 'ox‑herd' or a descriptive epithet). Grammatically the clause reports what the steward was told.
segments.s033-g67: به مردی = 'among/unto men' (به + indefinite مردی); نشیند is a classical/static/optative third‑person form of 'نشستن' used here in a wishing or jussive sense ('may you sit' or 'you should sit'), and تو is the addressee 'you' placed after the predicate (poetic word order). به آرام is an adverbial phrase 'in peace / calmly' modifying نشیند.
segments.s033-g68: ز = 'from' (poetic ز for از). تاج و کمر = 'crown and girdle' (symbols of rank/office). بسترد is an older/poetic verbal form; here it functions to indicate removal or seizure of reputation (poetic nuance), and نام تو = 'your name/reputation' is the direct object. The whole mini‑clause says your name will be taken away/stripped from your crown and belt (i.e. you will lose honor).
segments.s033-g69: به آیین خویِ‌ش (به + آیین + خوی + ش) = 'to his own custom/way' (خوی...ش is the possessive 'his habit/custom'). آورد is the past 'brought/returned' and ناسپاس functions as a predicate/adjective 'the ungrateful one' — together: 'he brought the ungrateful one back to his own custom.'
segments.s033-g70: چنین‌گر is a conditional/adverbial compound 'if so / if thus'. The clause reads elliptically: 'If so (چنین‌گر), you (تو) — مهمان شناسی شناس —' where مهمان شناسی is a nominal compound 'knowledge/recognition of guests' and شناس is the agentive 'knower/expert'. Together: 'If that is so, you are an expert in recognizing guests.'
segments.s033-g71: بدو گفت ضحاک is inverted word order: بدو 'to him', گفت 'said' (poetic past), ضحاک 'Zahhāk' is the speaker; چندین منال follows as the object or scope 'many (such) utterances/requests/desires' — i.e. 'Zahhāk said many things to him.' Note classical Persian permits this flexible ordering.
segments.s033-g72: که introduces the reported content: مهمان گستاخ = 'an impudent/insolent guest' (noun + adjective). بهتر به فال = 'is better as an omen' or 'is a better omen' (بهتر = 'better', به فال = 'for omen' / 'as an omen'). The clause states Zahhāk's judgment that an insolent guest is a preferable omen.

sentences.s033-s53: All your bonds and stratagems were taken away by her beauty; Delaram seized him and entrusted your time.
sentences.s033-s5: Seated calmly in the presence, like a tall cypress; the moon turned away from her profile.
sentences.s033-s6: From one hand, the dark cypress Shahrnāz; in the other hand, moon‑faced Arnavāz.
sentences.s033-s7: The whole city was entirely full of his army; the girded men, drawn up in ranks, stood at his gate.
sentences.s033-s8: He neither became distraught nor asked the secret; praying, he went and performed his prayer.
sentences.s033-s13: The brave king commanded (him): “Go and seek the regalia of the royal throne.”
sentences.s033-s14: Prepare wine and summon the entertainers; fill the cup and adorn the banquet.
sentences.s033-s15: Anyone who deserves my solace is (such); in learning he is that very one who repels my heart.
sentences.s033-s16: Bring the assembly and set it on my throne; let it be such as befits my fortune.
sentences.s033-s17: When the kadhkhudā heard this speech from him, he did what the guide had told him.
sentences.s033-s18: They brought bright/illuminating things and the comforters; the same they placed in his dish along with the jewels of the chiefs.
sentences.s033-s19: Fereydun cast off his grief and chose repose; one night he held a feast such as was fitting.
sentences.s033-s20: When the world became peaceful, the incense‑runners hastened about; one bearing incense came forth from before the new commander.
sentences.s033-s25: Of those three, one was the smaller (or younger) among them, lofty as a cypress and with the kingly countenance.
sentences.s033-s26: He is younger in years, yet his abundance/excellence is greater than those elders; he sets his foot before them.
sentences.s033-s27: One (of them) bears a mace like a single bare mountain; it/ he keeps shining in the midst of the company.
sentences.s033-s28: They came on horseback into the king’s iwan; two substantial men came with him — Hamīdūn — on the road.
sentences.s033-s37: Thus he gave him this reply, 'Kandro,' and added: 'Indeed I heard; you, hear the answer.'
sentences.s033-s38: If this renowned one is your guest, what business has he in your bedchamber?
sentences.s033-s39: That he sits with Jam's world-bearing daughters and frames counsel about matters great and small.
sentences.s033-s40: With one hand he takes Shahrnaz's face; with the other, the agate-lipped Arnavaz.
sentences.s033-s29: He came to the Kayanian throne and sat upon it; he made all your bonds and stratagems base.
sentences.s033-s30: Anyone who was in your hall, whether from the men of men or from your diwan (court/devils).
sentences.s033-s31: Their heads entirely fell from their feet; all the brains were mingled with blood.
sentences.s033-s32: Zahhak said to him: 'Perhaps (it is) a corpse — since the guest was merry, one ought [to treat] the body.'
sentences.s033-s21: He mounted (took his seat) on his pack and turned his face toward King Zahhak.
sentences.s033-s22: No sooner had he come than he reached before the spahbed; he told, at length, whatever he had seen and heard.
sentences.s033-s23: He said to him, 'O king, the rebels — signs of their return have appeared.'
sentences.s033-s24: Three proud men, with troops, came up from another country.
sentences.s033-s41: He makes the dark-hued night itself worse than that; beneath his head he lays a pillow of musk.
sentences.s033-s42: Like musk are those two locks of hair — your two moons — which had always been your delight.
sentences.s033-s43: He clasps them to his chest when he grows half-drunk; a guest should not be handled in this way.
sentences.s033-s44: Zahhak grew angry and sent (off) a bird; he heard those words that had wished for death.
sentences.s033-s9: He praised him, “O king — may you live forever, so long as time endures.”
sentences.s033-s10: May an auspicious seat be yours, O noble one, for you are worthy of imperial kingship.
sentences.s033-s11: May the seven realms of the world be your servants; may your head be higher than the rain‑bearing cloud.
sentences.s033-s12: He addressed Fereydun and ordered him to go forward; he then openly made all his secret plain.
sentences.s033-s49: If you are deprived of the moon's auspicious hour, how will you give me the means to accomplish my work?
sentences.s033-s50: Why don’t you attend to your own work? For this enterprise has never before come to pass.
sentences.s033-s51: Out of the crown of greatness you came forth like a hair from dough; Mahtara, take (seek) a remedy.
sentences.s033-s52: An enemy came upon you and at the time sat down; one with a massive, ox‑like mace in his hand.
sentences.s033-s45: With vile curses and a harsh cry, let astonishment surge with the tumult of fate.
sentences.s033-s46: He said to him, 'You shall never, in my house, be my guardian from now on.'
sentences.s033-s47: Thus the attendant gave him this reply: 'Do not suppose, O king, henceforth...'
sentences.s033-s48: that by that fortune you will never, for my sake, be such as to give me the office of town-chief.
sentences.s033-s1: When the realm was emptied of Zahhâk, there was a certain rich man, ill‑fated in the manner of a wayfarer.
sentences.s033-s2: Who had treasure and a throne and a house — marvels, through the pity of the village headman.
sentences.s033-s3: They called it kandru by name; slowly he advanced, taking a step toward oppression.
sentences.s033-s4: Kandro rushed into the palace; in the iwan he saw a certain new merchant.
sentences.s033-s33: He thus gave the answer to the steward, saying that the guest refused the ox‑herd's cudgel.
sentences.s033-s34: May you sit calmly among men; from crown and girdle your name will be taken away.
sentences.s033-s35: He restored the ungrateful one to his own custom; if so, you are a connoisseur of guests (i.e. you recognize guests well).
sentences.s033-s36: Zahhāk said to him many things, that an impudent guest makes a better omen.
